title: A note on chat interfaces before the story settled
summary: A post about chat interfaces before the story settled, written against the temptation to make the future sound cleaner than it is.
topic: chat interfaces before the story settled
body: My notes on chat interfaces began with a small irritation in July 2022. I was struck by how quickly the larger story hardened into common sense, and that hardening made me suspicious. Quick agreement often means someone has already done the trimming required to make the thought portable. In this case the omitted piece was what gets lost when the demo becomes the argument. What looks abstract in prose becomes very specific in life: upkeep, harm absorption, trust order, and who gets to market that order as progress.

Chat interfaces appealed to me because it sat right on the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. Engineering makes for easier conversation because it offers parts, systems, metrics, and a tidy performance surface. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Each tool contains a small politics of the user and the surrounding world, whether it announces that politics or not. If you pay attention, those assumptions are right there in the design. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also about what kind of daily conduct the system quietly asks people to adopt.

What mattered most was the mismatch between prototype life and public life. The prototype has one advantage, nobody mistakes it for settled reality. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who signed up for weirdness. It is much riskier to move the same logic into schools, hospitals, transit, housing, or workplaces and assume a sleek interface will do the moral work. What gets lost when the demo becomes the argument becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

The real shift was not in my taste for the subject but in how little vague language I was willing to tolerate. After writing “The problem with tool-using models is usually how novelty can impersonate depth,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. I trust claims about the future only when they can survive the messier terms of actual life. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. The part worth watching is the moment a helpful system begins to act like a dependency. Chat interfaces seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. Usually what hardens is not the tool itself but a pattern of relationship around it.

A final note to myself from July 2022: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. What stays in focus for me now is not the headline forecast but the burden a tool pushes onto ordinary people. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

references.title: The problem with tool-using models is usually how novelty can impersonate depth
I keep coming back to warehouse software
